params.text:
Using the right equipment

The safest way to reduce handling risk is often to let equipment take the strain. Common aids in care include hoists, slide sheets, transfer boards and handling belts. Equipment only makes things safer when it is the right type, in good order, and used by someone who has been trained and assessed as competent.

Never improvise. Using the wrong sling with a hoist, or a piece of equipment you have not been shown how to use, can seriously injure the person you support.

params.alt: A carer helping a person to stand safely

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Get to know the main handling aids used in care. Tap each card to see what it does.

dialogs.answer: Lifts a person who cannot bear weight, or raises someone from the floor. The correct size and type of sling must be used, and staff must be trained. Hoists and slings fall under LOLER.
A low-friction sheet that helps reposition someone in bed — moving them up, or turning them — without dragging their skin or straining your back.
A smooth board that bridges a gap, for example between a wheelchair and a bed, so a person can slide across rather than being lifted.
A belt with handles worn by a person who can partly support themselves, giving you a secure hold to steady or guide them — never to lift their full weight.

params.question: It is fine to use any hoist sling as long as it fits under the person.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not true. You must use the correct type and size of sling for the specific hoist and person, as set out in their care plan and your training.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Slings must be the correct type and size for that hoist and that person. The wrong sling can let someone slip or fall and cause serious injury.
